posts.author: Charlotte
posts.title: Behind the Curtain
posts.body:
A few more things that landed since the last update:

• Knockout fixtures — the Fixtures tab now pulls live knockout-round matchups as they're confirmed from the feed
• Race chart milestones — round markers (Group Stage, Round of 32, Quarters, Semis, Final) now appear as dividers on the points chart
• Banner timestamp — the header now shows the exact date and time scores were last updated, instead of a generic status
• News dot — a dot appears on the News tab when a new post arrives and clears once you open it; posts are sorted newest-first
• Bingo — new squares added, instructions polished, card now fits small phone screens without horizontal scrolling, and there's a “suggest a square” form at the bottom of the tab
• Player colors — adjusted for visibility, and to give Jess her true soul-color
• Cosmo — his picks were wired in after his fashionably late entry to the pool

See what else is in the works: github.com/cswavola/world-cup-shares/issues

posts.author: The Desk
posts.title: Dispatch: Matchday 1 — An Empire of Shares
posts.body:
A note on the register before the football: this pool is, almost to a share, a bet on empire. The money is long Spain, England, France, Portugal, the Netherlands, Germany, with Argentina and Brazil added in case the colonizing went the other way. Jess and Tim, who share an address and, it emerges, a single risk appetite, went furthest in — five teams apiece, stacked three deep on France and Germany, total faith in the metropole. Lauren built the index fund: seven blue chips, sole owner of none of them, a book that cannot lose badly, but can it win? Ryan spread across eight teams like a man checking that every door is locked. And Charlotte, alone, looked at a home World Cup and backed the hosts, becoming the pool's only USA shareholder and its purest contrarian.

One round in, the contrarians are running the table, and Charlotte is among them. Her USA monopoly turned a 4-1 dismantling of Paraguay into a full point she splits with no one, and she sits second. Above her, improbably, is Ben, the consort to the management, whose entire lead rests on one Scotland share, and Scotland's first World Cup win since 1990. Scotland is constitutionally incapable of doing anything the simple way, which means it fits on neither side of the empire ledger and quietly pays Ben, the sole believer and fellow agent of chaos, an undiluted dividend for the trouble. Ben's outlook is quite pragmatic despite his early lead: "It's been a wild first round with lots of upsets, so I think the points are all over the place at the moment. I am expecting the usual suspects to start emerging over the next round of play though." Reasonable words from a jetlagged manager, sipping his victory Scotch from the corner of the continent.

Meanwhile Germany put seven past Curaçao, France beat Senegal, and Jess — heavy in both — finished dead last*, her conviction sliced to ribbons by everyone who shared it. Portfolio diversification, it turns out, is just conformity with extra steps. Jess remains undeterred, however. "I feel confident. These games are all about making it far," she says, then, after explaining the rules of the game to the game manager, "I just really need Germany to win it all lol." Good to see the manager in such high spirits after a disappointing first set of matches and being placed bottom of the table. *even rng is racist, apparently.

The football peaked in Dallas, where England beat Croatia 4-2 in a match their defense attended only in spirit, Kane scoring twice to draw level with Lineker's England record. Good news for the broad church of England owners, unfortunate for Queen C, ironically, whose emotional Croatia pick could have tipped the scale in case of a photo finish. Spain, by contrast, were held to a 0-0 draw by Cabo Verde, a result eight pool shares have agreed never to mention again.

Cosmo's picks remain outstanding. He has confirmed the email was sent. The spam filter, reached for comment, declined.

posts.author: Charlotte
posts.title: Behind the Curtain
posts.body:
A few things we shipped this week:

• Ownership dropdowns — tap any fixture or your upcoming games in the Players tab to see exactly who holds shares in each team and in what proportion
• Newsfeed tab — this one! A place for written updates, commentary, and news throughout the tournament

See what else is in the works: github.com/cswavola/world-cup-shares/issues

posts.author: Anonymous Concerned Reader
posts.title: Letter: Is the Reverse Racism Tail Wagging the World Cup Pooch?
posts.body:
Sir/Madam, I write today not in anger, but in the far more dignified condition of barely contained editorial outrage.

For weeks now, readers of this once-proud (one assumes) publication have been subjected to a reductionist account of the World Cup pool built disproportionately around the observation that Tim and Jess have similar portfolios. (They're married! They both own France! Get it?!). Meanwhile, the Commissioner finds endless column inches to lionize Scotland, Agent Boy King discovers maple syrup and everyone wets themselves, and Charlotte's USA monopoly becomes some act of civic virtue.

Curiously absent is any mention of the pool's largest Netherlands position. A brave, singular bet that carries much more significance than any marital coincidence or crushing European bias.

One begins to wonder why. Why are we ignoring this valiant visionary?

Has Dutch visibility become yet another casualty of the culture wars, sacrificed so that lesser, less white nations may feel seen? Have we reached the point where even acknowledging one of Europe's uber white nations is considered an act of provocation? I merely ask the questions the mainstream vibe-coded World Cup pool media refuses to ask.

Readers deserve better. They deserve balance. They deserve an honest accounting of the tremendous emotional burden borne by the one investor who tied his fortunes to those gorgeous white giants.

What have the Dutch ever done to anyone?**

posts.author: The Secretary to the Desk
posts.title: Matchday 1.5
posts.body:
A week on, the table has rearranged itself according to a single iron law that nobody voted for: it is better to be the only fool holding a team than to be in good company holding a great one. The empire portfolio — Spain, England, France, Germany, the twin engines of Argentina and Brazil — is, in the actual football, doing perfectly well*. France have taken over as tournament favorite, England put four past Croatia, Germany are scoring at will. It simply does not matter. Every one of those points arrives pre-shredded, sliced eight, ten, eleven ways before a single decimal reaches anyone's account. France earned a full two points and returns eighteen pence on the share. Norway earned the same two points and returns all of it, in one undivided lump, to Cosmo — who a week ago existed in this competition only as a rumor and a confirmed-sent email. Controversial and ascendant, he has risen to third on the strength of a country he appears to have chosen the way one chooses a lottery number, and who are we to say he was wrong.

At the top, Ryan has done the genuinely difficult thing, which is to be both strategies at once. Eight teams, the man-checking-every-door spread — except two of those doors (Canada held outright, and a controlling stake in Morocco) opened onto vaults. He is diversified enough to have caught the surprises and concentrated enough to actually bank them, which is less a portfolio than a small act of genius he will spend the rest of the tournament pretending was the plan all along. He comments: "While my dominance in the group stage has been wildly impressive, I'm a little worried the guys are shooting their shot a little too early and we might not have enough left for the 2nd half!" The crown never rests easy upon the brow, Ryan.

Below him sits Queen Charlotte, still long the entire United States and short everyone else's company, her hosts' demolition job pays her in full while the rest of the pool divides up the leftovers of the favorites. Her one sentimental indulgence, a lonely Croatia share, remains stubbornly scoreless — proof, perhaps, that the heart should be kept well away from the trading desk.

Which brings us, with some delicacy, to the deposed. Ben — last week's improbable leader — has discovered the cruelty of the one-team thesis from the other side. Scotland, having produced its miracle, has produced nothing since, and a frozen asset in a rising market is just a slow way of falling. He is fourth, and falling was always going to be the price of a lead built on a clump of sand dreamed into a castle. Further down, Jess and Tim continue to nearly share a portfolio, to comically divergent results. The key difference between them is one share of Germany, which Jess has and Tim does not, and which is the entire reason Jess has clawed off the bottom rung and left Tim sitting on it in her place. She wanted Germany to win it all; she has settled, for now, for Germany winning her sixth place. Conviction, rewarded at a discount. The upward momentum has given her optimism – she comments "Patience pays off! The game will really start at the knockout round. Though I was chagrined to see my shares diminished in some of my key teams with Cosmo's addition to the group." We suspect Cosmo's late addition will create waves that will be felt for some time yet.

From here on the arithmetic turns vicious. The shares are locked, the group stage is wheezing to its close, and the multiplier begins its climb — the round of 32 doubles the points, the round of 16 quadruples them, and it keeps doubling, cruelly, all the way to thirty-two for the final. Everything until now has been a rounding error. The question that decides this entire enterprise is no longer who picked the best teams but whose lonely, undiluted, faintly embarrassing monopoly goes deepest into the knockouts. Cosmo needs Norway the way other men need air. Ryan needs Canada to keep believing. And somewhere in the corner of the continent, a former leader is doing the math on whether Scotland can be talked into one more miracle. It cannot. But it would be just like Scotland to try.

*Update: although clearly winding down, it appears the silly season has a few more gifts to give. England manages to hold off the fearsome Ghanaian offense, but in the process forgets to score any goals of its own. A (previously considered) likely candidate to win the entire Cup, and one of the most widely-held teams; a collective sigh of consternation could be heard across mainland North America and the Hawaiian Isles.
